items.q: What is the difference between 'medium' and 'technique'?
items.correct: Medium is the material used (e.g. oil paint); technique is how it is applied (e.g. impasto).
items.misconception: Medium and technique mean the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Medium refers only to the size of the canvas.
items.why: Students use the terms interchangeably, blurring what a work is made of with how it was made.

items.q: In analysis, what does 'composition' refer to?
items.correct: How elements are arranged within the work — placement, balance and focal points.
items.misconception: The materials the artwork is composed of.
items.random_wrong: The music playing in the gallery.
items.why: The phrase 'composed of' misleads students; composition concerns arrangement, not materials.

items.q: Does 'analysing' an artwork mean saying whether you like it?
items.correct: No — analysis examines how formal elements create effects and meaning, supported by visual evidence.
items.misconception: Yes — analysis is your personal opinion of the work.
items.random_wrong: No — analysis means measuring the frame.
items.why: Students substitute preference for analysis; opinion only counts when justified through evidence in the work.

items.q: Which word best describes the range of colours used?
items.why: Correct answer: Palette. “Composition” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Medium. “Palette” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many key critical terms are introduced in this foundation?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
